research_answer: No — the resource-based economy explanation doesn't hold up when you compare Chile directly to other natural resource exporters. Canada, Australia, and New Zealand are also heavily resource-endowed, but Chile lags significantly behind all three in export sophistication metrics. Chile is far more specialized in raw materials, forestry, and agriculture than these advanced natural-resource exporters, and it underperforms dramatically in manufactured goods, which tend to be more sophisticated and higher-wage. Chile is also highly under-specialized in all categories of non-resource-based exports, particularly high-technology products, whereas countries like Canada and Australia have successfully diversified into more complex manufactured exports alongside their resource base. So Chile's predicament isn't inherent to being resource-rich — it reflects a structural failure to move beyond the most peripheral, low-sophistication parts of the export basket.
supporting_quotes: Furthermore, this isn't due to Chile's status as a natural resource-based economy, as the country lags in these dimensions even when compared to countries like Canada, Australia, and New Zealand.

Chile is clearly much more specialized in raw materials, forestry, and agriculture than these advanced natural-resource exporters. And as can be seen below, the country is lagging in manufactured goods, which tend to be more sophisticated and pay higher wages.

Again, we see that Chile is highly specialized in primary products. Moreover, it is highly under-specialized in all categories of non-resource based exports, which tend to be more sophisticated and pay higher wages. This is particularly true of the higher-technology product categories.
